payload.summary: The weekly review pulse reveals a mixed sentiment towards the app, with 30% of users praising its user experience and interface, while 25% criticise the fees, charges, and rewards. Technical issues and support (20%) and security, privacy, and features (25%) are also major concerns. Many users have reported issues with cashbacks, withdrawals, and forex markups, while some have praised the customer support and user-friendly interface.

action_items: Improve the cashback and rewards system to increase user satisfaction
Enhance the technical support and troubleshooting process to reduce user complaints
Review and revise the forex markup and fee structure to make it more competitive and transparent
action_ideas: Improve the cashback and rewards system to increase user satisfaction
Enhance the technical support and troubleshooting process to reduce user complaints
Review and revise the forex markup and fee structure to make it more competitive and transparent

explanation_bullets: A mutual fund exit load is a fee charged to investors when they redeem their units from a mutual fund scheme.
The exit load is typically expressed as a percentage of the redemption amount.
It is deducted from the redemption proceeds and paid to the mutual fund house.
The purpose of an exit load is to discourage investors from withdrawing their money too quickly, especially during times of market volatility.
The exit load percentage and period vary across different mutual fund schemes.
Investors should check the mutual fund scheme's documents to understand the exit load structure before investing.
A brokerage fee is a charge levied by a broker or investment firm for facilitating a transaction, such as buying or selling securities.
The fee can be a flat rate or a percentage of the transaction amount.
Brokerage fees can vary depending on the type of investment, the size of the transaction, and the broker or firm.
These fees are typically deducted from the investor's account at the time of the transaction.
Brokerage fees can apply to various types of investments, including stocks, bonds, mutual funds, and exchange-traded funds (ETFs).
Investors should review their account statements and transaction confirmations to understand the brokerage fees associated with their investments.
